mechanism_explained: Agents infer canonical files from filesystem structure; context rot makes broad reading costly.
Folder taxonomy separates source, current, generated, archived, and indexed project files so agents can navigate safely.
Manifest tiers implement progressive disclosure for just-in-time context loading.
One canonical rules file plus thin or generated adapters prevents cross-tool instruction drift.
Context cost separates into fixed, per-task, accumulated, tool-output, connector/MCP, and ordering/cache concerns.
State files externalize durable decisions so fresh sessions do not lose continuity.
Compaction preserves selected state; clearing resets; fresh sessions isolate tasks or reviewers.
Portable core files plus tool-specific adapters and ignore-file conventions make one folder usable across multiple agents.
MCP servers add tool-surface and payload costs that should be scoped and aggregated.
Workflow patterns separate roles and agent-friendly CLIs make work testable and reduce self-grading.
Archive-not-delete, deterministic guards, and large-file rules reduce filesystem blast radius.
Recurring workflow failures cluster around weak project, source, session, and tool boundaries.
Failure diagnosis maps symptom to pattern, trigger, mitigation, and implementation order rather than guessing fixes.
Least privilege constrains filesystem, shell, network, MCP, and ignore-file assumptions to task-sized blast radius.
Rules files and adapters are measurable artifacts whose benefits must exceed context and maintenance cost.
